V100.title: Perplexity acquires Read.cv, a social media platform for professionals
V100.author: TechCrunch Staff
V100.excerpt: Perplexity acquired Read.cv, leading to the platform's wind-down beginning January 17, 2025. Andy Chung described the acquisition as 'incredibly bittersweet,' stating the team believed 'great things happen when the world's knowledge is made more open and accessible.' Perplexity CEO Aravind Srinivas noted the team was 'incredibly capable in designing and building consumer and social experiences.'
V100.relevance: Primary source on Read.cv shutdown and Perplexity acqui-hire — key dead product case study
V100.credibility: high — TechCrunch, major tech publication, contemporaneous reporting

V101.title: Read CV Acquired by Perplexity | Hacker News
V101.author: Various HN commenters
V101.excerpt: Users help grow a product with their network, their content, or their money, get comfortable with that product, integrate it into their lives... and then it's yanked out. Finding and building a community for (tech) creatives is hard. Adding networking and post sharing aspects was stellar.
V101.relevance: Community reaction to Read.cv shutdown — reveals what users valued and the platform risk perception
V101.credibility: medium — user comments, representative of tech community sentiment but not primary data

V102.title: Peter Johnston on X: Shutting down Polywork
V102.author: Peter Johnston (@multiplay3r)
V102.excerpt: Building consumer social is like doing a startup on Level 900 Difficulty Mode... Unfortunately, we were unable to sustain our growth and no longer saw a viable path to Polywork becoming a gigantic business. I still really believe the world needs a new standard in professional identity and discovery.
V102.relevance: Founder's own words on Polywork shutdown — primary source on why it failed
V102.credibility: high — direct founder statement, primary source

V103.title: What Happened To Polywork? An Eye-opening Story Of Fail Growth
V103.author: Elena (Prompt-Led Product)
V103.excerpt: Polywork launched in 2021 with an ambitious vision to challenge LinkedIn's dominance... By 2022, the company secured $13 million in funding from notable investors including the Collison brothers, Reddit co-founder Alexis Ohanian, and product strategist Elad Gil... The 2023 transformation alienated early adopters who embraced the original networking vision.
V103.relevance: Detailed post-mortem analysis of Polywork's failure — covers funding, pivots, user reaction
V103.credibility: medium — newsletter analysis, secondary source, but well-researched

V104.title: Polywork Shuts Down | Hacker News
V104.author: Various HN commenters
V104.excerpt: If you're not in the US and you try it, chances are you'll find very few users from your own region. They started as LinkedIn competitor but ended up becoming a website builder. Too many page builders already and chasing profit for VCs has killed another good idea.
V104.relevance: Community reaction to Polywork shutdown — reveals regional network effect problem and pivot criticism

V105.title: Stripe-backed Irish LinkedIn-challenger Polywork to close
V105.author: Business Post
V105.excerpt: Polywork, the professional network that took on LinkedIn, will close its doors on January 31, 2025. The startup was unable to sustain its growth and no longer saw a viable path to becoming a gigantic business.
V105.relevance: News reporting on Polywork closure with funding and shutdown details

V106.title: Contra: The Commission-Free Freelance Platform Changing the Game in 2025
V106.author: JoWorks Studio
V106.excerpt: Contra has grown to over 1 million independent professionals worldwide. The user base grew by 20% in Q1 2025. In 2025, Contra launched Indy AI, an artificial intelligence-powered tool that fundamentally changes how freelancers find work.
V106.relevance: Contra 2025 status — shows how it survived by repositioning as commission-free marketplace
V106.credibility: medium — studio blog, secondary analysis source

V107.title: 75+ LinkedIn Statistics to Capture More Leads in 2024
V107.author: SocialPilot
V107.excerpt: LinkedIn has over 1.01 billion users globally with a growth of 120 million in the past year. 90%+ of recruiters use LinkedIn as their primary talent sourcing tool. Over 16 million users activated Creator Mode. Creator posts get 35% more reach. Thought leadership posts generate 6x more engagement than job-related content.
V107.relevance: LinkedIn statistics for 2024 — platform usage and creator mode data
V107.credibility: medium — aggregated statistics, cite with caution on exact numbers

V108.title: Framer vs Webflow 2026: An Expert's Practitioner Guide
V108.author: FlowNinja
V108.excerpt: Framer is a design-first tool ideal for freelancers or creatives focused on visuals and quick launches. Framer goes for a more design-centric approach similar to Figma. For individual designers, design-led brands, and small marketing teams running fewer than 50 pages, Framer's velocity is hard to beat.
V108.relevance: Framer vs Webflow comparison for designers — context for personal website platform choice

V109.title: Personal Website vs. LinkedIn: When You Need Both and What to Put on Each
V109.author: CareerBldr
V109.excerpt: 90%+ of recruiters use LinkedIn as their primary talent sourcing tool. 70% of employers check candidates' online presence during the screening process. Your LinkedIn profile is for reaching your network and being discovered, while your personal website is for showing your depth and owning your story.
V109.relevance: LinkedIn vs personal website data — key framing for how professionals should use each
V109.credibility: medium — career advice site, cites Jobvite survey

V110.title: Dribbble vs. Behance for Designers in 2024
V110.author: Giant Creates
V110.excerpt: Behance is open to everyone, leading to diverse portfolios, and designers can create multi-page project case studies with full stories and processes. Dribbble started as a platform for designers to share 'shots' — small previews or snippets of ongoing work. Use Behance for your full projects and Dribbble to tease visuals.
V110.relevance: Behance vs Dribbble for designers — usage patterns and who uses which

V111.title: Web3 Publishing Platform Mirror Sells to Paragraph, Pivots to Social App 'Kiosk'
V111.author: CoinDesk
V111.excerpt: Mirror, the Ethereum-based publishing tool, is shutting down, with all users and content migrating to Paragraph. Paragraph acquired Mirror in 2024 and raised $5M from USV and Coinbase Ventures.
V111.relevance: Mirror.xyz acquisition by Paragraph — web3 publishing consolidation event
V111.credibility: high — CoinDesk is primary source for crypto industry news

V112.title: Is this the end of 'Build in Public'? Here's why top indie hackers are suddenly disappearing.
V112.author: Indie Hackers community
V112.excerpt: Successful indie hackers stopped sharing revenue numbers due to concerns about sharing becoming bragging, competitive disadvantages, personal safety, and negative relationship impacts. Shift from full transparency to 'stealth mode' above $10-30k MRR due to higher stakes.
V112.relevance: Evolution of Build in Public movement — why top earners stopped sharing publicly
V112.credibility: medium — community discussion, represents practitioner sentiment

V113.title: How Nomad List hit $5.3M revenue and 29K customers in 2024
V113.author: Latka (SaaS metrics)
V113.excerpt: Nomad List hit $5.3M in revenue in 2024. Pieter Levels' full portfolio includes Photo AI at $132K/month, Interior AI at $38-45K/month, Nomad List at $38K/month, and Remote OK at $35-41K/month, totaling around $250K+/month.
V113.relevance: Pieter Levels revenue data — confirms build in public economic model
V113.credibility: medium — Latka collects self-reported metrics, directionally reliable

V114.title: Justin Welsh | Building a portfolio of one-person businesses to $5M
V114.author: Justin Welsh
V114.excerpt: 472M+ impressions, $12M in profit, six years of lessons. Most people are building their business for someone else. Don't build a brand. Live a life.
V114.relevance: Justin Welsh personal brand model — LinkedIn-first solopreneur case study
V114.credibility: medium — self-reported, but widely cited and verifiable through public LinkedIn

V115.title: andrewchen.com — Andrew Chen personal site and essay archive
V115.author: Andrew Chen
V115.excerpt: Andrew Chen has written hundreds of essays on the topics of startups and user growth. Before joining a16z he had built a blog with 650-plus essays on user growth, viral loops, churn curves, and network effects that became required reading across Silicon Valley's growth and product communities.
V115.relevance: Andrew Chen cross-platform identity example — personal site + Substack + LinkedIn
V115.credibility: high — primary source, the site itself

V116.title: thesephist.com — Linus Lee personal site
V116.author: Linus Lee
V116.excerpt: Linus maintains a collection of over 100 programming projects at thesephist.com, where he's also been blogging for almost a decade. Notable projects include Monocle (universal personal search engine) and Oak (programming language).
V116.relevance: Linus Lee personal site as engineer identity model — GitHub + personal site as front door

V117.title: Home — Patrick Collison personal site
V117.author: Patrick Collison
V117.excerpt: patrickcollison.com includes sections for About, Advice, Blog, Bookshelf, Culture, Enlightenment, Fast, Growth, Labs, Links, Novels, People, Pollution, Progress, Questions, Solar, and SV history. His contact email is patrick@collison.ie.
V117.relevance: Patrick Collison as engineer-executive personal site model — curated knowledge sections as identity

V118.title: The Curiosity Chronicle — Sahil Bloom on Substack
V118.author: Sahil Bloom
V118.excerpt: Lenny Rachitsky's newsletter has 1.2M+ subscribers and is ranked #1 in Business on Substack. Rachitsky posts 1-2 episodes a week on his podcast. Newsletter makes over $500k per year.
V118.relevance: Sahil Bloom Substack as professional identity anchor — newsletter writer cross-platform model

V119.title: The 5 Biggest Takeaways From Lenny's Journey To 1 Million Subscribers
V119.author: Growth in Reverse
V119.excerpt: Lenny Rachitsky's newsletter has 1.2M+ subscribers, ranked #1 in Business on Substack. Newsletter makes over $500k per year. He hosts a podcast that makes more money than he made in corporate America.
V119.relevance: Lenny Rachitsky platform model and financial success data

V120.title: Reid Hoffman LinkedIn Profile
V120.author: Reid Hoffman
V120.excerpt: Co-Founder, LinkedIn, Manas AI & Inflection AI. Founding Team, PayPal. Author of Superagency. Podcaster of Possible and Masters of Scale.
V120.relevance: Reid Hoffman as foundational example of LinkedIn-as-professional-identity

V121.title: Wellfound (formerly AngelList Talent) — Startup Job Search
V121.author: Wellfound
V121.excerpt: Wellfound has a vast network of over 10 million startup-ready candidates and more than 150,000 tech job listings. Mixed reviews as of 2024-2025: 'used to be a good place to find employment but has started feeling more like a graveyard.'
V121.relevance: Wellfound/AngelList status as startup job-seeking professional identity platform
V121.credibility: medium — platform's own claims, mixed with Trustpilot user reviews

V122.title: Day Trading Attention: Gary Vee's 2024 Blueprint For Building Brand and Sales on Social Media
V122.author: Gary Vaynerchuk / Young and Profiting podcast
V122.excerpt: In 2024, Gary's focus has shifted to a concept he calls 'Day Trading Attention,' which centers on understanding consumer attention shifts and leveraging emerging platforms. You need to understand pop culture and the latest trends.
V122.relevance: Gary Vee 2024 personal branding philosophy — evolution of hustle-culture personal brand framework

V123.title: Bento.me — Link in bio tool (now redirects to Linktree)
V123.author: Linktree
V123.excerpt: Bento.me is no longer available, and existing accounts have been permanently deleted. The service was previously acquired by Linktree, but appears to have been discontinued as a standalone platform.
V123.relevance: Bento.me death — link-in-bio market consolidation, second-tier players killed
V123.credibility: high — observed from the platform itself

V124.title: LinkedIn Creator Mode Features (now integrated, toggle removed March 2024)
V124.author: LinkedIn
V124.excerpt: LinkedIn removed the Creator Mode on/off toggle from profiles in March 2024, and you no longer 'activate Creator Mode' by toggling it on; instead you access and set up each tool directly. Features – like newsletters, enhanced analytics, and AI-driven audience matching – are fully available.
V124.relevance: LinkedIn Creator Mode evolution — important platform change for professional identity display

V125.title: DHH — David Heinemeier Hansson personal site
V125.author: David Heinemeier Hansson
V125.excerpt: DHH is the creator of Ruby on Rails and Omarchy, co-founder and CTO of 37signals (maker of Basecamp and HEY). His personal site at dhh.dk discusses open-source work, noting companies built with Rails including Github, Shopify, Airbnb, Square, Coinbase, and Zendesk.
V125.relevance: DHH as engineer-executive personal site model — personal site + X + GitHub

V126.title: How Marc Lou, PH's maker of the year, makes $50k+ every month
V126.author: Indie Hackers
V126.excerpt: Marc Lou was Product Hunt's maker of the year 2024. His earnings disclosed publicly: $124,772 in January 2025. Marc continues to drop his monthly numbers publicly, making him one of the most well-known names in the game.
V126.relevance: Marc Lou as build-in-public archetype — X + Product Hunt as primary professional identity

V127.title: The SEO MBA — Tom Critchlow on Substack
V127.author: Tom Critchlow
V127.excerpt: Tom Critchlow publishes 'The SEO MBA,' a Substack publication with tens of thousands of subscribers. His consulting practice is anchored in his personal site tomcritchlow.com and the newsletter as relationship deepeners.
V127.relevance: Tom Critchlow as consultant cross-platform model — personal site + newsletter
